title: 2 Kings
author: Unknown; Jewish tradition attributes it to Jeremiah
setting: The ministries of Elisha and later prophets, the fall of the northern kingdom of Israel to Assyria (722 BC), the reforms of Hezekiah and Josiah, and the fall of Jerusalem to Babylon (586 BC).
purpose: To show that the covenant curses of Deuteronomy 28 inevitably came upon a nation that persisted in idolatry, and that even godly kings like Hezekiah and Josiah could only delay, not avert, the judgment earned by generations of apostasy.
themes: The fulfillment of Deuteronomic covenant curses
Josiah's reforms and the book of the law
The Babylonian exile as covenant judgment

outline.label: Manasseh to the Fall of Jerusalem

after.label: Exile in Babylon; Ezekiel and Daniel ministries

key_people.name: Elisha
key_people.role: Elijah's successor who receives a double portion of his spirit; his miracles — raising the dead, feeding multitudes, cleansing a leper — are the most concentrated cluster of Elijah-like wonders in the OT

key_people.name: Hezekiah
key_people.role: One of Judah's greatest kings; his prayer against Sennacherib and his subsequent miraculous deliverance is the book's central example of what faith in crisis looks like

key_people.name: Josiah
key_people.role: King who discovers the Book of the Law and leads Judah's most thoroughgoing reform; his early death despite his faithfulness raises hard questions about the relationship between obedience and outcomes

key_people.name: Elijah (translated)
key_people.role: Taken up to heaven in a whirlwind at the opening of the book (ch. 2); his translation without death, and the expectation of his return (Mal 4:5), makes him a unique eschatological figure

key_people.name: Jehu
key_people.role: Anointed to destroy Ahab's house; his zeal for YHWH is genuine but ruthless, illustrating that reform without a changed heart produces limited and temporary results

context: 2 Kings spans 853–586 BC, covering the fall of both kingdoms: Israel to Assyria in 722 BC and Judah to Babylon in 586 BC. The book was almost certainly compiled in its final form during the Babylonian exile, and its purpose is explicitly theological: to explain why God allowed the destruction of Jerusalem and the exile of his people by tracing the long history of covenant violation by king after king.
christ_connection: Elisha's miracles are the most concentrated cluster of signs in the OT pointing forward to Jesus: he raises the Shunammite's son (2 Kgs 4), feeds 100 men with 20 loaves with food left over (4:42–44), and cleanses the Gentile Naaman's leprosy (ch. 5) — each of which Jesus echoes in his own ministry. Jesus himself points to the Elijah-Elisha cycle (Luke 4:24–27) as the precedent for his mission to Gentiles. The remnant preserved through the Assyrian and Babylonian catastrophes points to the remnant that survives judgment through the cross.

key_verses.note: The theological verdict on the fall of the northern kingdom: 'This occurred because the people of Israel had sinned against the LORD their God' — exile explained as covenant judgment
Hezekiah's prayer: 'Save us from his hand, that all the kingdoms of the earth may know that you alone are the LORD God' — the model of prayer that seeks God's glory, not merely personal deliverance
The discovery of the Book of the Law during Josiah's temple repairs — the catalyst for Judah's last great reformation
'So Judah was taken into exile out of its land' — the stark, final sentence reporting the fulfilment of every covenant curse Moses had warned of in Deuteronomy

themes_detail.title: Covenant Faithfulness and the Inevitability of Judgment
themes_detail.text: The structure of 2 Kings is a prolonged, painful documentation of Deuteronomy's warnings coming true. King after king in both Israel and Judah 'did what was evil in the sight of the LORD,' and the author counts each as walking in the sin of Jeroboam or failing to remove the high places. The fall of Samaria (722 BC) and the fall of Jerusalem (586 BC) are not presented as military accidents but as the precise fulfilment of the covenant curses God had warned about for centuries — making 2 Kings one of the most solemn pieces of historical theology in Scripture.

themes_detail.title: The Prophetic Witness as the Last Line of Defence
themes_detail.text: In the absence of faithful kings, the prophets become the primary channel through which God speaks, warns, and occasionally breaks in with grace. Elisha's ministry is almost entirely grace: he heals, feeds, raises the dead, and serves as the nation's invisible army. Isaiah advises Hezekiah. The writing prophets (Isaiah, Micah, Hosea, Amos) are contemporary with 2 Kings and provide the theological commentary on the events narrated. The book implicitly asks: what if the kings had listened to the prophets?

themes_detail.title: Reform, Revival, and the Limits of External Change
themes_detail.text: Hezekiah and Josiah are the two great reforming kings of Judah, and their reforms are presented as genuine and important. But both are followed by reversal: Manasseh's reign of 55 years undoes Hezekiah's reforms so thoroughly that even Josiah's efforts cannot avert the coming exile (23:26–27). The implication, which the prophets will make explicit, is that external reformation cannot substitute for the inward transformation God must perform. The exile itself becomes the painful surgery that prepares a remnant for a new covenant.

themes_detail.title: The Remnant: Hope Through and Beyond Judgment
themes_detail.text: Even in the darkness of 2 Kings, the book preserves signs of grace: the 7,000 who had not bowed to Baal (1 Kgs 19:18), Hezekiah's miraculous deliverance, Josiah's tender heart, Jehoiachin's release from prison in Babylon (25:27–30) — the final, deliberately inconclusive note on which the book ends. The Davidic line has not been extinguished; the exile is not the final word. This is the theological seed from which the prophets' promises of restoration, and ultimately the NT's announcement of the son of David, grow.
